does: This paper introduces CURE, a framework for removing sensitive knowledge from language models through post-hoc output correction rather than model retraining. The core method deploys a lightweight corrector module that inspects model outputs for leakage of target knowledge, retrieves relevant unlearning targets from a database as in-context references, and rewrites outputs into safe alternatives when sensitive information is detected. The corrector operates as a verification-and-revision layer applied to the original model's outputs, enabling adaptation to new unlearning requests without additional training. Evaluations demonstrate that CURE substantially outperforms prior input-based suppression approaches in eliminating underlying knowledge while maintaining scalability across large-scale unlearning scenarios.
connects: This work responds to the limitations of existing machine unlearning methods that primarily target input queries or rely on retraining model weights, which often fail to eliminate fundamental memorization and struggle with scalability. The paper builds on retrieval-augmented generation techniques to leverage in-context learning, allowing the corrector to adapt dynamically without gradient updates. It extends prior work on knowledge suppression and privacy protection in language models by shifting from preventing sensitive outputs at query time to detecting and correcting leaked knowledge in model responses. The approach also incorporates insights from output verification and conditional text generation, combining these techniques to create a modular solution that complements rather than replaces the base model.
enables: This framework opens pathways for practical, scalable unlearning in deployed language models where retraining is prohibitively expensive. The retrieval-augmented correction approach suggests future work on more sophisticated detection mechanisms that identify subtle forms of knowledge leakage beyond surface-level matches. It enables handling of dynamic unlearning requests where new sensitive information can be added to exclusion lists without model updates. The modular corrector design could extend to other safety and alignment challenges beyond unlearning, such as filtering harmful content or enforcing behavioral constraints. Additionally, this work points toward hybrid architectures combining lightweight post-hoc modules with frozen models, potentially applicable to other parameter-efficient safety interventions in large language models.
